questions.question:
"I cannot support this proposal. My _________ will not permit it."

A. conscious  B. consensus  C. conscience  D. consent
questions.to_find: the most appropriate word to complete the sentence about a personal moral sense

understand.plain: The speaker refuses to support a proposal because their inner moral sense forbids it. The blank requires a noun that refers to a person's internal ethical guide. All four options sound similar but differ sharply in meaning, so careful vocabulary knowledge is key.

keywords.explain: the inner sense of right and wrong that guides a person's moral decisions
keywords.example: His conscience prevented him from lying to his friend.

keywords.explain: an adjective meaning aware or awake; NOT a noun for a moral faculty
keywords.example: She was conscious of the noise outside.

keywords.explain: general agreement among a group of people
keywords.example: The committee reached a consensus after debate.

keywords.explain: permission or agreement given by a person
keywords.example: Parental consent is required for the trip.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a vocabulary question.

given.aim: fill the blank so the sentence expresses that the speaker's moral faculty opposes the proposal

terms.meaning: the faculty that distinguishes right from wrong for an individual
terms.example: Feeling guilty is your inner moral guide speaking.
terms.connects: directly maps to the word 'conscience'

terms.meaning: aware or in a state of wakefulness; cannot be 'will not permit'
terms.example: I was conscious of my mistake.
terms.connects: grammatically wrong as a noun in this slot

terms.meaning: consent = permission; conscience = moral sense — homophone trap
terms.example: You need consent to borrow it; conscience tells you if you should.
terms.connects: common confusion pair in vocabulary questions

given.plan: Look for the noun that personifies an inner ethical authority that can 'permit' or 'not permit' actions. That is always 'conscience'.

steps.title: Parse the sentence structure
steps.apply: The subject of the subordinate clause is the blank — it is a noun that 'will not permit'. An inner faculty that has such authority is the moral sense.
steps.note: Only a noun meaning the moral faculty fits grammatically and semantically here.

steps.title: Eliminate distractors
steps.apply: conscious: adjective, not a noun that can 'permit'. consensus: group agreement, irrelevant here. consent: permission given to others. conscience: the moral inner sense — matches perfectly.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a vocabulary-based verbal aptitude question.

questions.question:
If ' --> ' denotes increasing order of intensity, then the meaning of the words [charm --> enamor --> bewitch] is analogous to [bored --> ________ --> weary]. Which one of the given options is appropriate to fill the blank?

A. jaded  B. baffled  C. dead  D. worsted
questions.to_find: the word of intermediate intensity between 'bored' and 'weary' in a synonym intensity series

understand.plain: The question uses an analogy of escalating emotional intensity. 'Charm', 'enamor', and 'bewitch' represent mild, medium, and strong forms of fascination. By the same pattern, the blank must be a word stronger than 'bored' but weaker than 'weary' — all related to being fed up or exhausted with something. 'Jaded' fits as it means cynically tired from over-exposure.

keywords.explain: feeling tired, bored, or lacking enthusiasm, typically from having too much of something
keywords.example: After years of travelling, she grew jaded and stopped enjoying new destinations.

keywords.explain: feeling or showing tiredness, especially from great exertion or prolonged exposure — stronger than jaded
keywords.example: The soldiers were weary after days of marching.

keywords.explain: confused or puzzled; unrelated to the bored-tired spectrum
keywords.example: The scientist was baffled by the unexpected result.

keywords.explain: defeated or got the better of; unrelated to fatigue or boredom
keywords.example: He was worsted in the debate.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a vocabulary analogy question.

given.aim: identify the intermediate-intensity synonym in the fatigue/boredom series between 'bored' and 'weary'

terms.meaning: charm --> enamor --> bewitch mirrors bored --> ? --> weary
terms.example: Like charm is the mildest form of fascination, bored is the mildest form of being fed up.
terms.connects: the blank must sit between bored and weary in meaning-strength

terms.meaning: worn out in interest or feeling; stronger than bored but not as deep as weary
terms.example: A jaded reader has read so many books that none excite them anymore.
terms.connects: fills the middle slot in the bored-jaded-weary gradient

terms.meaning: these belong to entirely different semantic fields (confusion and defeat)
terms.example: Being baffled is about confusion, not fatigue.
terms.connects: eliminated because they break the semantic coherence of the series

given.plan: Match the semantic intensity gradient. Bored < jaded < weary is the natural escalation of tiredness/disillusionment.

steps.title: Establish the reference gradient
steps.apply: charm (mild attraction) --> enamor (moderate love) --> bewitch (intense spell). The analogous series is: bored (mild disinterest) --> ? --> weary (deep fatigue/exhaustion).
steps.note: The blank must be a word meaning disillusionment or deeper boredom, between bored and weary.

steps.title: Evaluate each option for the middle-intensity slot
steps.apply: A. jaded: disenchanted from over-experience — stronger than bored, lighter than weary. FITS. B. baffled: confused — wrong field. C. dead: colloquial for exhausted, but too extreme; also slang, not a clean mid-step. D. worsted: defeated — wrong field. Choose A.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a verbal analogy and vocabulary question.

questions.question:
People were prohibited _____ their vehicles near the entrance of the main administrative building.

A. to park  B. from parking  C. parking  D. to have parked
questions.to_find: the grammatically correct preposition-gerund combination after 'prohibited'

understand.plain: The verb 'prohibit' has a fixed collocation in English: it is always followed by 'from + gerund (verb-ing)'. This is a grammar question testing knowledge of verb + preposition patterns. Options with infinitives ('to park', 'to have parked') do not collocate with 'prohibit'.

keywords.explain: the standard English collocation; 'prohibited from doing something' means prevented or forbidden from doing it
keywords.example: Students are prohibited from using phones in the exam hall.

keywords.explain: the -ing form of a verb used as a noun (e.g., 'parking' in 'from parking')
keywords.example: Swimming is good exercise. / He was stopped from smoking.

keywords.explain: 'prohibit to park' is grammatically incorrect in standard English
keywords.example: Correct: prohibited from parking. Incorrect: prohibited to park.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a grammar and word usage question.

given.aim: complete the sentence with the correct verb-preposition pattern after 'prohibited'

terms.meaning: the only grammatically accepted pattern with the verb 'prohibit'
terms.example: Drivers are prohibited from parking on the pavement.
terms.connects: directly points to option B: 'from parking'

terms.meaning: used with verbs like 'allow', 'want', 'ask' — NOT with 'prohibit'
terms.example: They asked us to park in the designated area.
terms.connects: eliminates A and D

terms.meaning: 'prohibited parking' sounds incomplete without 'from'
terms.example: 'Prohibited parking' can be a noun phrase (sign), but not the right structure here.
terms.connects: eliminates C

given.plan: Apply the rule: prohibit + from + gerund. Match to the options.

steps.title: Recall the collocation rule for 'prohibit'
steps.apply: In English grammar, 'prohibit' takes the pattern: prohibit + object + from + V-ing. So: 'People were prohibited from parking'.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply: A. to park — wrong preposition pattern for 'prohibit'. B. from parking — correct gerund phrase. C. parking — missing required preposition 'from'. D. to have parked — perfect infinitive, wrong structure. B is correct.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a grammar (verb collocation) question.

questions.question:
Stock markets _______ at the news of the coup.

A. poised  B. plunged  C. plugged  D. probed
questions.to_find: the most appropriate verb describing stock market behaviour upon receiving alarming news

understand.plain: A coup (sudden overthrow of a government) is politically destabilising news. Stock markets react to uncertainty by falling sharply. The blank needs a word that conveys a sharp, dramatic fall or drop — exactly what 'plunged' means. The other options (poised, plugged, probed) do not describe a market reaction to bad news.

keywords.explain: fell steeply and suddenly — the standard financial journalism term for a sharp market drop
keywords.example: The index plunged 400 points after the central bank's announcement.

keywords.explain: ready and prepared, or balanced — suggests expectation, not reaction
keywords.example: The athlete was poised to break the record.

keywords.explain: blocked or connected; has no standard meaning in the context of stock markets
keywords.example: He plugged in the charger.

keywords.explain: investigated deeply; used for inquiry, not market movement
keywords.example: Investigators probed the financial scandal.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a vocabulary question on stock market language.

given.aim: identify the verb that correctly describes a sharp downward reaction of stock markets to destabilising news

terms.meaning: a sudden, illegal seizure of power from a government — highly destabilising
terms.example: Following the coup, foreign investors withdrew funds immediately.
terms.connects: establishes the context of fear and uncertainty that drives markets down

terms.meaning: to fall suddenly and steeply; commonly used in financial reporting
terms.example: Market indices plunged as political tensions escalated.
terms.connects: the only option meaning a sharp downward movement

terms.meaning: none of these describe a market response to news; they belong to different contexts
terms.example: Poised = prepared; plugged = blocked; probed = investigated.
terms.connects: all three are eliminated by context mismatch

given.plan: Match the context (bad political news causing fear) to the correct verb (sharp fall = plunged).

steps.title: Understand the context
steps.apply: A coup creates extreme political uncertainty. Investors react by selling, causing markets to drop sharply.
steps.note: The expected verb must mean 'fell sharply'.

steps.title: Match options to context
steps.apply: A. poised — means ready/balanced, not a reaction. B. plunged — sharp sudden fall, fits perfectly. C. plugged — blocked, irrelevant. D. probed — investigated, irrelevant. B is the answer.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a contextual vocabulary question.

questions.question:
As you grow older, an injury to your __________ may take longer to __________.

A. heal / heal  B. heal / heel  C. heal / heal  D. heel / heal
questions.to_find: the correct homophones: body-part noun for the first blank and recovery verb for the second blank

understand.plain: This question tests homophones — words that sound identical but have different spellings and meanings. 'Heel' (noun) is the back part of the foot; 'heal' (verb) means to recover from injury. The sentence says: an injury to your [heel] (back of foot) may take longer to [heal] (recover). Answer D places the noun 'heel' first and the verb 'heal' second.

keywords.explain: the rounded back part of the human foot
keywords.example: She developed a blister on her heel from the new shoes.

keywords.explain: to become healthy or whole again after injury or illness
keywords.example: The wound took two weeks to heal completely.

keywords.explain: words that have the same pronunciation but different meanings and spellings
keywords.example: 'Heel' and 'heal' are homophones; so are 'there', 'their', and 'they're'.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a homophone vocabulary question.

given.aim: select the correct pair of homophones where the first is a body part (noun) and the second is a verb meaning to recover

terms.meaning: requires a noun referring to part of the body that can be injured
terms.example: An injury to your heel (back of the foot).
terms.connects: 'heel' (noun) is needed in the first slot

terms.meaning: requires a verb meaning to recover or get better from an injury
terms.example: The injury took longer to heal.
terms.connects: 'heal' (verb) is needed in the second slot

terms.meaning: options A, B, C appear to offer combinations — only D correctly pairs heel (noun) then heal (verb)
terms.example: Option A 'heal/heal' uses a verb in the noun slot, which is wrong.
terms.connects: eliminates A, B, C

given.plan: Identify which blank needs a noun (body part) and which needs a verb (recovery), then match to the correct homophone for each.

steps.title: Analyse the grammatical role of each blank
steps.apply: Blank 1: 'an injury to your ___' — needs a noun (a body part). Blank 2: 'may take longer to ___' — needs a verb (an action/process).
steps.note: Body part = heel; recovery verb = heal.

steps.title: Select the correct homophone pair
steps.apply: D. heel / heal: first blank = heel (body part noun), second blank = heal (recover, verb). This is the only option with the correct noun-verb assignment. Options A and C both use 'heal/heal' — a verb in the noun slot. B uses 'heal/heel' — inverts the correct order.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a homophone/vocabulary question.

questions.question:
He is known for his unscrupulous ways. He always sheds ________ tears to deceive people.

A. fox's  B. crocodile's  C. crocodile  D. fox
questions.to_find: the correct adjective form of the idiomatic expression for insincere tears

understand.plain: The idiom 'crocodile tears' means insincere or fake expressions of sorrow used to manipulate others. The word 'crocodile' here acts as an attributive adjective modifying 'tears', so it should appear without a possessive apostrophe-s. The correct phrase is simply 'crocodile tears', not 'crocodile's tears'.

keywords.explain: a fixed English idiom meaning false or insincere tears, shown to manipulate others; based on the myth that crocodiles weep while eating their prey
keywords.example: The politician shed crocodile tears at the press conference but his policies showed no empathy.

keywords.explain: a noun used directly before another noun to describe it, without a possessive apostrophe
keywords.example: 'Chicken soup', 'dog food', 'crocodile tears' — 'chicken', 'dog', 'crocodile' are attributive nouns.

keywords.explain: having no moral principles; not honest or fair
keywords.example: The unscrupulous merchant sold counterfeit goods.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is an idiom and grammar question.

given.aim: select the idiomatically correct modifier for 'tears' that means fake/insincere tears

terms.meaning: the complete fixed expression meaning insincere tears; 'crocodile' is used as an attributive adjective without possessive marking
terms.example: He shed crocodile tears when caught.
terms.connects: the noun 'crocodile' (no apostrophe-s) is the correct modifier

terms.meaning: 'crocodile's tears' uses a possessive (belonging to a crocodile) whereas 'crocodile tears' uses an attributive noun (type of tears) — the idiom requires the attributive form
terms.example: 'Lion's share' is possessive; 'crocodile tears' is attributive.
terms.connects: eliminates option B (crocodile's)

terms.term: fox — not part of the idiom
terms.meaning: 'fox tears' or 'fox's tears' is not a recognised English idiom for deceitful weeping
terms.example: 'Sly as a fox' is an idiom, but not 'fox's tears'.
terms.connects: eliminates A and D

given.plan: Recall that 'crocodile tears' is a fixed idiom using 'crocodile' as an attributive noun (no possessive). Match to option C.

steps.title: Identify the relevant idiom
steps.apply: The context — unscrupulous person shedding tears to deceive — directly invokes the idiom 'crocodile tears'. This is a well-known phrase meaning fake, manipulative weeping.
steps.note: 'Fox tears' is not a recognised idiom.

steps.title: Choose the grammatically correct form
steps.apply: In the idiom 'crocodile tears', 'crocodile' is an attributive noun — no apostrophe. A (fox's) and B (crocodile's) both use possessives. D (fox) is the wrong animal. C (crocodile) — attributive noun — is correct.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is an idiom and grammar question.

questions.question:
After playing ________ hours of tennis, I am feeling ________ tired to walk back.

A. too/too  B. too/two  C. two/two  D. two/too
questions.to_find: the correct homophones: numeral for the first blank and degree adverb for the second blank

understand.plain: This question tests the homophones 'two' (the number 2) and 'too' (meaning also, or to an excessive degree). The first blank follows 'playing' and needs a number of hours, so it takes 'two' (numeral). The second blank modifies 'tired' in the sense of 'excessively tired', so it takes 'too' (adverb of degree). The correct combination is two/too — option D.

keywords.explain: the number 2; used to count or quantify
keywords.example: I waited for two hours at the airport.

keywords.explain: used before adjectives/adverbs to mean 'excessively' or 'more than enough'
keywords.example: It is too hot to go outside today.

keywords.explain: two/too/to all sound identical in spoken English but serve completely different grammatical functions
keywords.example: 'To' is a preposition; 'too' is an adverb; 'two' is a numeral.

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a homophone grammar question.

given.aim: fill both blanks with the correct member of the homophone set {two, too, to}

terms.meaning: the first blank comes before 'hours', requiring a numeral
terms.example: After playing two hours of tennis.
terms.connects: 'two' (number) fills blank 1

terms.meaning: the second blank modifies the adjective 'tired', meaning excessively; requires an adverb of degree
terms.example: I am feeling too tired to walk.
terms.connects: 'too' (excessive degree) fills blank 2

terms.meaning: 'too hours' is ungrammatical because 'too' cannot quantify a countable noun
terms.example: Incorrect: after playing too hours.
terms.connects: eliminates option A

given.plan: Blank 1 needs a numeral (two = 2). Blank 2 needs an adverb meaning excessively (too). The answer is D: two/too.

steps.title: Determine what each blank requires
steps.apply: Blank 1: 'playing ___ hours' — needs the number 2 (written as 'two'). Blank 2: 'feeling ___ tired' — needs an adverb of excess ('too = excessively').

steps.title: Match to the options
steps.apply: A. too/too — 'too hours' is wrong. B. too/two — 'too hours' is wrong. C. two/two — 'feeling two tired' is wrong. D. two/too — 'two hours' (numeral) and 'too tired' (excessive degree) both correct.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a homophone/vocabulary question.

questions.question:
Writing too many things on the _________ while teaching could make the students get _____________.

A. bored/board  B. board/bored  C. board/board  D. bored/bored
questions.to_find: the correct homophones: teaching surface noun for the first blank and disinterest adjective for the second blank

understand.plain: This is a homophone question using 'board' (the flat surface, like a blackboard or whiteboard used in teaching) and 'bored' (feeling uninterested or tedious). The first blank refers to the surface a teacher writes on — that is 'board'. The second blank describes how students feel — disinterested — that is 'bored'. So the correct pair is board/bored — option B.

keywords.explain: a flat surface used for writing in a classroom — blackboard, whiteboard, chalkboard
keywords.example: The teacher wrote equations on the board.

keywords.explain: feeling weary and uninterested due to lack of stimulation
keywords.example: Students become bored when the lecture is too monotonous.

keywords.explain: 'board' and 'bored' sound identical but serve different grammatical roles
keywords.example: 'Board' is a noun; 'bored' is an adjective (past participle used predicatively).

understand.visual_alt: No visual needed; this is a homophone vocabulary question.

given.aim: identify which blank needs the classroom surface ('board') and which needs the emotion of disinterest ('bored')

terms.meaning: 'writing things on the ___' requires a noun for the surface; that is 'board'
terms.example: Teachers write notes on the board.
terms.connects: 'board' (noun) fills blank 1

terms.meaning: 'make the students get ___' requires an adjective meaning uninterested; that is 'bored'
terms.example: Students get bored when there is too much writing.
terms.connects: 'bored' (adjective) fills blank 2

terms.meaning: A reverses the order (bored/board), C and D repeat the same word twice — none match the sentence meaning
terms.example: 'Writing on the bored' is nonsensical; 'students get board' is also nonsensical.
terms.connects: eliminates A, C, D

given.plan: First blank needs a classroom noun (board); second blank needs an emotion adjective (bored). The answer is B.

steps.title: Identify roles of the two blanks
steps.apply: Blank 1: 'writing on the ___' — a physical surface in a classroom. That is 'board'. Blank 2: 'students get ___' — an emotional state due to monotony. That is 'bored'.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply: A. bored/board — 'writing on the bored' is meaningless. B. board/bored — correct both ways. C. board/board — 'students get board' is meaningless. D. bored/bored — 'writing on the bored' is meaningless. B is the answer.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula is needed; this is a homophone/vocabulary question.
